rows.v: Cod, Haddock, Alaskan Pollock, Tilapia, Pangasius (Swai), Whiting
Mild, flaky, and forgiving — great for breading, baking, or simple sautés.
The whiter a fish's raw flesh, the milder it usually tastes — a simple rule that lets you guess almost any unfamiliar fish on a menu: pale flesh means mild, deeply colored flesh means bold.
Halibut, Flounder, Sole, Turbot, Plaice
Delicate, fast-cooking fillets — best with gentle methods like pan-searing or poaching.

Flatfish like sole and flounder are born looking like normal fish, with an eye on each side — then, as they grow, one eye slowly migrates around the head so the fish can lie flat on the seabed with both eyes facing up.
Swordfish, Mahi-Mahi, Grouper, Monkfish, Branzino (Sea Bass), Chilean Sea Bass, Red Snapper
Dense enough to grill, sear, or skewer without falling apart.
'Chilean sea bass' is a marketing invention — the fish is actually the Patagonian toothfish, renamed in the 1970s because no one wanted to order something called a toothfish.
Mackerel, Sardine, Anchovy, Herring, Bluefish, Sablefish (Black Cod)
Rich in omega-3s with bold flavor — stand up to grilling, smoking, and strong seasonings.

Oily fish carry their fat throughout the flesh rather than in the liver, which is exactly why they're so rich in omega-3s — and why mackerel, sardines, and herring spoil faster and taste 'fishier' than lean white fish.
Atlantic Salmon, King (Chinook) Salmon, Sockeye Salmon, Rainbow Trout, Steelhead Trout, Arctic Char
Versatile and forgiving — roast, grill, cure, or smoke with great results.
Wild salmon get their pink-red color from a diet of krill and shrimp; farmed salmon would be grey without it, so their feed includes the same natural pigment to turn the flesh pink.
Bluefin Tuna, Yellowfin (Ahi) Tuna, Albacore Tuna, Skipjack Tuna, Yellowtail (Hamachi), Kanpachi
Best served rare or seared — overcooking dries out these lean, meaty fish quickly.

Tuna are warm-blooded — among the few fish that can raise their body temperature above the surrounding water — which lets them swim fast enough to cross entire oceans and gives the prized bluefin its rich, marbled belly.
Catfish, Walleye, Yellow Perch, Carp, Striped Bass
Mild, approachable flavors — classic for frying, blackening, or simple pan dishes.

A fish's flavor depends heavily on its water — many freshwater fish like catfish and carp can taste 'muddy' from compounds absorbed from their environment, which is why cooks soak them or rely on clean, cold, fast-moving water.
Sturgeon Caviar, Salmon Roe (Ikura), Flying Fish Roe (Tobiko), Capelin Roe (Masago), Trout Roe
Served cold, in small amounts, with simple accompaniments that don't compete with the briny pop.

Only the salted roe of sturgeon may legally be called 'caviar' — everything else, from salmon ikura to flying-fish tobiko, is technically just 'roe,' however luxurious; and true caviar is always served with a non-metal spoon, since metal taints its delicate flavor.
